title: A Mehxico Story
body:
Mexico had the honour of opening the festival of football and, in true Roman style, they needed a sacrifice. The hapless lambs were South Africa. Every World Cup I find myself wondering whether this will be the year African nations finally show their strength and depth. Unfortunately, if South Africa are anything to go by, the answer remains a firm no.

They looked out of their depth from the first whistle and Mexico should have been several up by half time. Against better opposition, El Tri might have rued those missed chances — but South Africa never really looked like making a game of it. A second goal in the second half, bookended by a flurry of red cards (three in total, a World Cup opening game record), made the second period watchable if not exactly a classic. Mexico cruised.

title: Good Korea, Bad Korea
body:
I only caught the highlights of the day's second game — which may become something of a recurring theme — but it looked like another dominant first half from the nominal home side. South Korea racked up almost as many missed chances as there were empty seats in Guadalajara (also likely to be a recurring theme), and went in at the break with nothing to show for it.

The second half started brightly for South Korea, but Czech Republic somehow fashioned a goal against the run of play. Luckily for those of you with the good Korea in your picks, they levelled things up before substitute Oh Hyeon-gyu popped up to secure the winner in the closing stages. A Soucek header that briefly threatened to nick it back for the Czechs was ruled out for offside, and that was your lot.

title: No They Can'tada
body:
Day two saw our remaining hosts get their tournaments underway with mixed results.

Canada went behind early and spent the rest of the game trying to claw it back. Throughout, they looked the side more intent on actually playing football, while Bosnia and Herzegovina were perfectly happy to sit back, soak up pressure, and wait for something to happen on the break. It was exactly one of those breaks that saw them burst clear and take the lead.

Canada continued to push, but their waves of attack kept crashing against a wall of defenders and rarely troubled the opposition's box.

The second half was more of the same — Canada creating, BiH sneaking. Canada's equaliser with ten minutes remaining felt like it would set up a breathless finale, but although both sides had chances, a draw felt like the right result after an entertaining match.

title: Surprisingly Fine
body:
South of the (Canadian) border, in the hallowed land of Soccer, the USA started with high expectations — and after this display, those expectations might actually be warranted. Their first half against Paraguay was some of the best football I've seen from the US team, and their 3–0 lead at the break felt thoroughly deserved.

Their opener — seven minutes in — was a horrible poke-in from a Paraguay defender, but it came at the end of some lovely build-up play. By the time Balogun added his second deep into first-half injury time, the US could stroll into the dressing room feeling very pleased with themselves.

The second half wasn't as convincing. The US eased off, made a few more mistakes, and some frailties at the back emerged that a better team might look to exploit. Paraguay did find the net with a well-worked goal the US defence should have dealt with more comfortably — but it was only a small speed bump on the road to a well-won victory. A fourth goal just before the final whistle was the bow on top.
footer: A personal footnote: while watching the 2002 World Cup with an American girl I was trying to impress, I made the drunken prediction that the US would reach a World Cup final within 25 years. This is — somewhat alarmingly — that window's last chance. I don't think it's going to happen. But they do look closer than I've seen them before.

title: A Match Worth Waiting For?
body:
As the media has frequently reminded us, it's been 28 years since Scotland made it to a World Cup. For Haiti the wait has been twice as long, and from the first whistle they were keen to make clear they hadn't travelled all this way just to be a footnote.

Every chance they got, Haiti came flying forward in numbers, with Scotland working hard to hold off the cavalry charge.

Scotland had their chances — not as many as they'd have liked — and it ultimately came down to a scuffed McGinn shot that deflected past the keeper to put them ahead. A goal on the board settled Scotland's nerves somewhat, though Haiti continued to threaten an equaliser right to the end.

Desperate for a cushion, Scotland probed for a second, but poor finishing and dogged defending kept Haiti in it until the final whistle.

Three points from their opening game was the bare minimum Scotland needed if they're to have any hope of reaching the knockouts. Morocco and Brazil are considerably sterner tests, and they're going to need to play a good deal better than this to squeeze out results that could get them through the group stage.

title: Scoring Is All That Matters
body:
Australia are one of those sides that always seems to promise a lot at World Cups and rarely delivers. Turkey were one of the teams I thought might outperform their PickerGame cost. We've established what I know.

Turkey applied pressure for 90 minutes. They had most of the ball, most of the chances... and Australia scored twice. And that, as they say, is how you take the points.

In a game that will not trouble the history books, a couple of individual moments separated two sides unlikely to trouble the sharp end of the knockout stages. Turkey will need to be more ruthless in front of goal. Australia will need to do, well, more — but they'll take it.

title: A Close Shave
body:
When I looked at the match schedule, the first fixture that leapt out at me was Brazil vs Morocco. A meeting between arguably the best sides from South America and Africa — hopefully a feast of football.

Out of the blocks it was Morocco who looked the sharper side. They had several chances and it was no surprise when a great through ball and a deft dink put them one up.

Going behind gave Brazil a nudge, though they still needed a moment of individual brilliance from Vinícius Jr. to claw their way back into it.

Brazil would have been relieved to go in level at the break, but any hopes of a change in fortune in the second half were swiftly dashed. Both sides made changes, Morocco continued to look the better team, and they'll feel they deserved more than a point — though a draw against the tournament favourites is a decent return. Brazil will be sending Vini Jr. and Alisson a very large fruit basket and quietly hoping the rest of the squad turns up for the next one.

title: Persistence Pays
body:
I've been saying in the build-up to this World Cup that Qatar would do better than expected. Well, even a broken cuckoo clock is right twice a day.

Not taking your chances has been a theme of this tournament already. While Canada had to settle for a point, Switzerland drawing with Qatar is the first genuine upset of the competition.

The Swiss had already squandered several clear chances before being awarded a penalty that wasn't — or at least shouldn't have been. It was well struck, but I'm going to need more proof it wasn't offside before I move on.

Switzerland continued to spurn chances for the remainder of the game, always looking the better side but never quite looking like they actually wanted to win it.

What Qatar lacked in talent they made up for in sheer bloody-mindedness — though it never really felt like it would pay off. When Lee Dixon said, two minutes into injury time, that there was still time for Qatar to grab a point, I filed it under "things pundits say." Reader, he was right.

With less than two minutes of injury time remaining, Qatar were on the board and seconds away from their first ever World Cup point. The Swiss finally found second gear. Too little, too late.

title: One Nil to the Team That Scored
body:
This was a game between two teams who felt defending was beneath them and shots on target were against the spirit of the game. For the majority of the ninety minutes both sides seemed perfectly happy to break with pace and then put the final shot wide.

Ecuador started the stronger but Ivory Coast soon found their way into the game.

One goal was always going to be enough to steal the points, and in the end it was Ivory Coast who managed to find the net — though "find the net" perhaps flatters both teams given the afternoon they'd had.

title: A View From The Sofa (Highlights Only)
body:
I turned on my TV at 8pm this evening, ready to watch Germany humble one of the many teams who should probably be at home watching. Thanks to the frankly baffling match schedule, all I got was adverts followed by the build-up to the next game.

So this is another match report based on ten minutes of highlights. Unlike the middle-of-the-night games — which no one watches and I can embellish freely — I have to be at least vaguely careful not to just make things up with this one.

This was always going to be one-sided, and when Germany went one up from a great strike six minutes in, expectations were duly set.
When Curaçao equalised, it felt like an M. Night Shyamalan twist — although much earlier in the story than usual. Were we about to see a repeat of Germany's group stage collapses from the last two World Cups?

Unfortunately for the tiny island nation, the celebrations for their first ever World Cup goal weren't destined to last. Germany were back on script with two goals before half time and another within minutes of the restart.

As the goals continued to flow, Curaçao fashioned a few chances but were never really going to get back into it. Hopefully, they can add to their tally before what looks likely to be a brief tournament run comes to an end.

For Germany, things can only get harder — but they'll be glad to have started strong and look every inch one of the favourites to be there when it matters.

title: Shower Timing: A Cautionary Tale
body:
The first 50 minutes of this clash weren't the most riveting football of the tournament. As it was getting late I decided to quickly jump in the shower before catching the last thirty minutes. Unfortunately this meant I missed all three goals.

The thing I find most surprising about the hydration breaks is that we just get to watch players drinking water. Surely they could at least show us a mini highlights package. Or failing that, some adverts. At least then I'd know what car I should be driving.

Netherlands are one of those teams who seem like they should be a footballing giant, but who always end up underperforming. Like Chelsea. Japan feel like they should be the pride of Asian football, but I'm not sure where that pride should rank in the world game.
Both teams should make it through to the knockouts but will be hoping for better performances.

Ok, I'll admit it. I was keen to get to bed so wrote this with 15 minutes left on the clock hoping for an easy life. Japan deserved that equaliser and hopefully that's the last time I need to update this.

title: Flat Pack Football
body:
Sweden are one of those teams I feel should do well but never quite expect to reach the semifinals. Tunisia I'll confess I know nothing about, so I genuinely can't tell you whether this scoreline was a shock or whether Sweden simply played a blinder.

Tunisia had some good attacks and deserved to get on the scoresheet, but even at 2-1 this never really looked like finishing in anything other than a Swedish win. Their front line looks sharp and I think they're going to punish any team that can't get their defence in order.

This was always going to be a tough group and I suspect Tunisia will struggle to pick up much from their remaining games.

title: A Point Each, Nobody Happy
body:
Uruguay were quick out of the blocks in a game they would have expected to win comfortably. Saudi Arabia sat deep and absorbed the pressure, though they were still grateful for the acrobatic reflexes of their keeper, who single-handedly kept them in the contest during the opening exchanges.

Saudi Arabia offered little going forward in the first half, but it was from one of their rare forays that they earned the corner, which, against the run of play, put them ahead.

The goal lit a fire under Uruguay, who turned the pressure up even higher. For a long period it looked like they'd run out of ideas, but they eventually forced the ball over the line with around ten minutes remaining.

Uruguay will be frustrated they couldn't convert their dominance into more. Saudi will be disappointed their dam was eventually breached after holding firm for so long. On balance, a draw was probably fair, though it'll feel different in each dressing room.

title: Cape Fear
body:
Just 10% of the way through the tournament and I think we've already seen the shock of the competition. Spain were my favourites to lift the trophy next month, but a resolute defensive display earned Cape Verde a point that will be remembered for years.

Spain were languid — and although they spent much of the game camped on the edge of the Cape Verde penalty area, truly threatening forays into the box were few and far between. Even the introduction of their hero in waiting couldn't make enough of a difference.

That's not to say Cape Verde's keeper didn't pull off some impressive saves to keep his side in it — he did, and deserves enormous credit.

Spain will need to do considerably better, though qualifying for the next round should still be comfortable enough. Cape Verde will look at their upcoming fixture against Saudi Arabia and quietly start dreaming about sticking around in this competition longer than anyone expected.

title: Iran, NZ Ran, Everyone Ran
body:
Iran started fast and with intent, and for the opening exchanges the New Zealand defence looked like it might buckle at any moment. Lines were disorganised, clearances were nervous, and Iran smelled blood. Which made it all the more against the run of play when New Zealand got their name on the scoresheet first, a well-worked move that Chris Wood was instrumental in, finished coolly by a just who was alive to the opportunity.

The goal did little to settle either defence, and both sides continued to look generous at the back. Iran, to their credit, kept pressing and were rewarded just before half time with a deserved equaliser.

The second half followed much the same script. New Zealand retook the lead, Iran pegged them back, and for the final stretch both teams went in search of a winner, with chances at both ends deep into injury time keeping everyone on their feet.

Both teams showed some genuinely nice build-up play and constructed their goals well, though it's always easier to look good in attack when the opposition's defence is handing out time and space like free samples.

title: Belgian Waffle
body:
Belgium have been at or near the top of the world rankings for a decade, and not once in that time have I heard anyone say they were excited to watch them play or that they were favourites to win a tournament.
So although I went into this game expecting a Belgium win, I wasn't entirely surprised when Egypt went one up with a brilliant driven shot.

Egypt should have had a second midway through the second half, but it was Belgium who ended up with the next name on the scoresheet — and from that point the game never quite recovered its earlier spark.

Both teams had chances, but Egypt will probably feel the most hard done by with a draw.

title: Mmmbappé
body:
I spent much of half time writing this report in my head. I was going to talk about how little interest the French team had in defending, how Mbappé was looking past his best, how Senegal looked dynamic going forward while France looked like they'd rather be somewhere else entirely.

Then, ten minutes into the second half, the game came alive. The French attacking line woke up as if someone had flicked a switch, and the confidence the Senegal defence had shown for forty-five minutes quietly evaporated.

Chances came at both ends, but it was France who got on the scoresheet first, despite having a clear penalty denied even after the referee took a long, presumably educational, trip to the monitor. A brilliant counter-attack with ten minutes remaining put France two up and on cruise control.

By ninety minutes it felt like the result was settled. Then, in the 95th minute, Senegal pulled one back with a wonder strike and suddenly the possibility of something extraordinary raised its head.

That head was swiftly removed, less than a minute later, by another potential goal of the tournament from Mbappé.

One of the best games of the tournament so far ended with a win for one of the favourites. Senegal will rue their missed chances in the first half, which could have killed the game off before France had even laced their boots. France will hope they can sustain the pace and intensity of that second half, and if they can, they'll expect to be very much in the mix come the semi-finals.

title: The Haaland Show (And Supporting Cast)
body:
Today was a day for the superstars to shine, and Erling Haaland was handed his first opportunity to make his case for World Cup glory.

An exciting first half offered chances for both teams, but it was Haaland who struck first. He's the kind of player who seems to force goals into existence through sheer strength and willpower rather than coaxing them gently, but it works, and it's genuinely thrilling to watch.

Iraq were never going to let him have it all his own way, and their equaliser was thoroughly deserved. Unfortunately for them, a loose back pass to the keeper was all the encouragement Haaland needed to pounce and restore Norway's lead before the break.

In the second half Norway looked more settled and cohesive, and Iraq,  despite having their moments,  never truly looked like taking anything from the game. Norway's third and fourth goals were the clearest sign yet that this is not simply a one-man show. There is a team here that could go deep into this competition.

title: The Conductor
body:
Coming into this World Cup, I wondered whether it might be a tournament too far for the game's megastars. Would the names that all the world knows struggle to keep pace with fresher faces and younger legs?

Tonight suggested there is still plenty of life in some of the old dogs.

Let's not pretend otherwise, this was the Messi show. He was the conductor, coaxing a symphony out of the Argentine orchestra around him.

In the first half Algeria looked like they might make a game of it. They had the greater number and higher quality of chances, but Argentina had Messi, and over and over again he proved the difference. His first goal, a long-range strike the keeper will feel he should have done better with, was one of Argentina's few bright moments of the opening half. In the second half they found their rhythm, and although Algeria continued to threaten, the result never truly felt in doubt. It was simply a matter of when Messi scored next, rather than if.

His first World Cup hat trick is a statement, and a timely response to Mbappé's earlier performance this evening.

title: Weird Balls
body:
I don't know if it's something to do with the World Cup ball, but we've had a remarkable number of great long-range goals so far this tournament. It was one of those strikes that put Austria ahead against the run of play.

Despite having a bucketful of chances throughout the first half, it wasn't until the 50th minute that Jordan were able to score a thoroughly deserved equaliser, and for a spell it looked like they might go on and nick something from the game.

Unfortunately, no matter how well they played, Jordan were destined to leave empty-handed. An own goal and a very late penalty gave Austria all three points and sent Jordan home disappointed, probably wondering what might have been had they taken even a handful of those first-half chances.

title: Highlights? What Highlights?
body:
You can't always tell how good a match has been from the highlights. I once tried to explain to my wife, who grew up on baseball, that football isn't always about the goals. That a tense, hard-fought 0-0 could be just as exciting as a 4-3 ding-dong. I may have been exaggerating slightly, and it didn't work. She still can't stand it.

Watching the highlights of this match, my overwhelming impression was that neither side is going to trouble anyone in this tournament. Panama looked the stronger of the two, but only marginally, and never looked like fashioning anything from their attacks. Ghana didn't register a shot on target until the 50th minute, and I'm not sure they managed a second until deep into injury time.

I was fully expecting a goalless draw, which would have made progression extremely difficult for both teams, when in the 95th minute Ghana surprised me, and I'm guessing most people watching the highlights, with a tap-in from a well-placed cross. You could argue this was against the run of play, but that would imply there was any meaningful play at the other end to speak of.

The goal seemed to wake Panama up, and the final couple of minutes featured a few frantic half-chances that came to nothing.

Both teams will struggle to pick up much from their remaining fixtures. One of them might sneak through to the knockouts, but I'd be surprised if either goes any further than that.

title: Emphatic on Paper
body:
I was never that good at football, and no one has ever asked me to coach the England team. But if they ever do, my first act will be to fine anyone who attempts a stutter run-up for a penalty. Any novelty penalty technique, frankly. Zero tolerance.

Luckily Harry Kane was given a second chance to do the right thing, and ten minutes into their World Cup campaign England were on the scoresheet.

England looked considerably better going forward than at the back, and Croatia were quick to identify and pounce on that frailty to level things up.

Both sides continued to have chances, and it was Kane again whose powerful header put England back in front. Any hopes of going into the break with that lead intact were dashed right on the whistle by a brilliantly worked piece of Croatian team play that brought the game level again.

Jude Bellingham must have had a few choice words at half time, because he came out of the dressing room like a bullet. England were back on top within two minutes, and he should have had a second barely a minute later.
For the next ten minutes we saw the England we always knew they could be. Intense, relentless pressure that somehow Croatia managed to weather without conceding.
There was no way England could sustain that level, and as the second hydration break approached, Croatia began to push back. Into the unofficial fourth quarter, England started to look shakier. Croatia struggled to win possession but pressed forward, stifling England's attempts to play out from the back.

It wasn't until the 85th minute that England finally put the game to bed. A brilliant move saw substitutes Saka and Rashford combine to make it 4-2, and that was that.

It's a great result, but it's done little to settle my nerves about the defence. They looked like a back line still getting to know each other, which is a fairly damning indictment of Tuchel's insistence on rotating his options right up until the final training session. You pick a defence, you stick with it. Apparently not.

A win is a win, and on paper this looks emphatic. But the second half performance only papered over the cracks that were so visible in the first.

title: Congo-ing Nowhere
body:
Those of you who are PickerGame veterans may remember that I am not a member of the Ronaldo fan club. There are plenty of reasons, but it's probably easiest to just accept it as fact and move on. So when Portugal took an early lead, I was glad it wasn't He Who Should Not Be Playing on the scoresheet, but also quietly worried that this Portugal side might actually be quite good.

Luckily, those fears proved unfounded. That early goal was one of the few clear chances they'd find themselves with over the full ninety minutes.

DR Congo (does anyone else find it sounds genuinely awkward every time a commentator says that) had a number of chances throughout, but it wasn't until the last kick of the first half that they managed to find the back of the net.

In the second half Portugal had the majority of possession but never looked capable of regaining the lead. Ronaldo seemed content to stand around waiting for someone to roll the ball into his path, and his mere presence appeared to throw the rest of the team off balance, which is quite an achievement when you think about it.

Another game where neither team deserved to win, and neither will be happy with the draw or their performance.

title: Welcome to the World Cup, Uzbekistan
body:
It might be down to the editing, but from the highlights this match looked considerably more exciting than the Ghana vs Panama game earlier this morning. Alternatively, it could just be that there were actual goals.

Colombia were out of the blocks fast, and Uzbekistan, one of the tournament's debutants, looked like they might struggle to stay in it.

It took until the 40th minute for Colombia to get on the scoresheet, but rather than killing the game off, the goal acted as a wake-up call to the Uzbeks. When they levelled in the 60th minute, it didn't feel like they'd go on to take all three points, but at least they were making a game of it.

A goalkeeping error let Luis Diaz put Colombia back in front, and it felt deserved. A third goal in the 99th minute made the result look a good deal more comfortable than it had actually been.

title: Czech, Please
body:
I don't think there is a team in this tournament who can look less like they're going to win a game when they're 1-0 up than Czech Republic. Like a cat up a tree, they keep finding themselves in the lead with absolutely no idea how to get down again.

At no point in the game did South Africa look like they were going to salvage anything from this match, right up until the moment they did.

Neither team deserved any points. Unfortunately, that's not how football works. Or Pickergame.

title: Switzerland, Eventually
body:
For much of this game it felt like a slog. I know that's hard to believe from the scoreline, but scores can be deceiving.

Switzerland were clearly the better side and had the majority of the chances. BiH had a few breaks where they nearly fashioned something, but for most of the game it was the Swiss doing the pushing. For 73 minutes their attacks crashed upon a defensive wall, and on the rare occasions they did get through, the keeper was there to make the stop.

After the 74th minute, everything went a bit crazy.
Switzerland were finally rewarded for their persistence with a goal, which only encouraged them to push for more. Five minutes later, a reckless challenge on a Swiss player clean through on goal brought out the referee's red card, and any hope Bosnia had of clawing things back walked off the pitch and straight back into the dressing room.

A second and third goal followed in short order, and that felt like that. Until, four minutes into injury time, Bosnia found the back of the net with a sweet strike from the edge of the box. With barely a minute left, this should have been the footnote that ended my report. But another reckless Bosnian challenge, this time in the box and admittedly with minimal contact, had the referee pointing to the spot. Switzerland stepped up, scored, and reclaimed their thoroughly deserved three-goal margin.

title: O Canada
body:
After their first game results, I thought this might be a close one. Sometimes I'm wrong.

From early on the Qatari defence had no idea how to deal with the crosses Canada were flinging into the box, and the Canadians were absolutely determined to make them pay. They were dogged in chasing down every loose ball and parried shot, and it was one of those dropped saves that let them sneak the opener.

A second soon followed, and at that point the result felt sealed. When Canada thought they had a penalty the crowd were ready to celebrate a memorable 3-0, but after a lengthy VAR review the referee changed his mind, ruling the foul had been outside the box and awarding a free kick instead, along with a red card to the Qatari left back. I don't think either team was entirely happy with that outcome.

Nothing came from the free kick, but the extra man made an uneven match increasingly lopsided, and Canada grabbed a third before half time.

What looked like a fairly innocuous Qatari challenge ten minutes into the second half ended with a Canadian being carried off on a stretcher with a suspected broken leg and a second Qatari taking an early shower. It felt like one of those situations where the referee punishes the outcome rather than the action, but that is often the way.

Qatari heads dropped even further, their challenges got sloppier, and their defence was eventually reduced to walking pace. That Canada only added three more goals will almost feel like a relief.

title: A Win Is a Win (Just About)
body:
Mexico went into this game knowing that a win would earn them top of the group, early qualification to the knockouts, a last 32 game against a third place team, and home field advantage until at least the last 16 should they make it that far. In short, there was plenty to play for.

For much of the match it felt like one of those boxing bouts where neither fighter wants to throw the first punch. The Mexican goal, when it eventually arrived, was a gift from the Korean keeper. Poor communication with his centre back left him flapping at the ball, and Mexico were alert enough to capitalise and poke it into the net.

At that point Mexico appeared to decide they had quite enough goals, thank you, and for much of the remaining time it was South Korea making all the effort to change things, without ever quite finding the end product to do so.

They did have one late chance that threatened to sneak over the line, but the Mexican keeper was just about able to claw it away.

Mexico will be celebrating. Two wins, neither entirely deserved, and a very comfortable path to the knockouts. They will need to find another gear if they're to get past the last 32, but that's a problem for another day.

Korea need a win from their final group game, and will need to be considerably more ruthless with their chances when it comes.

title: Samba football
body:
After their sluggish performance against Morocco in their opening game, Brazil looked like they had arrived with a point to prove, both about scoring goals and about how they score them.

They started crafting chances from the off, and it was only due to a touch of sloppiness that it took until midway through the half to get on the scoresheet.

Their opening goal, while featuring some lovely build-up play, was more bundled over the line than delicately placed. But it's the scoring of goals that counts, not the aesthetics.

Brazil's next two, both scored before half time, were far more attractive finishes, and I imagine the dressing room at the break was a considerably more upbeat place than it was last week.
The second half was, by comparison, a bit of a damp squib. Brazil spurned a number of chances while their keeper was actually called upon to make a few saves. Haiti looked much better after the break, though how much of that was down to Brazil taking their foot off the gas is difficult to say.

That first half performance is the kind of flair we were all hoping for from Brazil. If they can sustain that form, they will make any potential opponent at least a little nervous.

title: Tartan Nightmare
body:
Haiti was always going to be the easiest of Scotland's World Cup fixtures, but even with most teams in this tournament able to progress, regardless of their opening result, they were always going to need something from at least one of their remaining two games.

Morocco, who both won and lost the most recent African Cup of Nations, were their next opponents and a considerably sterner test.

It was barely two minutes in when Morocco went one up, and Scotland were immediately left searching for a plan B.

Unfortunately, plan B turned out to be giving Morocco enormous amounts of space and hoping they'd waste their chances.

It was injury time before Scotland recovered from the early shock and managed to register anything that even resembled a chance.
The second half saw Scotland get more of the ball as they chased the equaliser that would have earned them the point they needed, but they were never quite good enough. Morocco continued to spurn chances, but in the end it didn't matter.

Scotland looked better in the second half than their dreadful first, but they will struggle to get anything from even the sluggish Brazil we saw last week. There is a chance they scrape through to the knockouts on three points, but it's not a hook I'd hang all my hopes on.

title: One Rule, One Red, One Goal
body:
Neither of these teams would have been happy with their opening performance, so both would have been hoping for a better showing and three points from this one.

Paraguay were quickest out of the blocks, and when their goal arrived it was fully deserved. Turkey had been close but always second best up until that point.

It was a tetchy match that for much of the first half felt like it was on the verge of exploding.

It was during one of those handbags moments that the strangest sending off of the tournament so far occurred, when a Paraguayan player became the first to be dismissed for covering his mouth while speaking.

For those of you who don't spend your lives obsessing about football, this stems from a new rule introduced after a recent incident in which a player was accused of using racially targeted insults while covering his mouth to prevent cameras from seeing what he was saying. What was said was disputed, so FIFA decided to limit the issue by banning the practice entirely.

Whether the rule itself is right is a conversation for another day. But the player knew it existed, and while it was clearly the heat of the moment, if you're not going to enforce rules there is no point having them.
With the extra man Turkey went all out attack, trying to salvage something not just from the game but from their World Cup campaign entirely.

I lost track of the number of chances they saw go begging, less because of excellent defending and more because of spectacularly sloppy finishing.

Paraguay did just enough to hold on and will go into their final game daring to dream about progressing.

Turkey will go home wondering what might have been, and quietly shopping for a new pair of shooting boots.

title: Comfortable, Then Less So
body:
By the time the US scored ten minutes in they had already fashioned several chances, while Australia were struggling to get out of the starting blocks. As the half progressed so did the American dominance, though there were signs from the Socceroos that they would at least put up a fight.

A second goal close to half time felt like it should wrap up the win and potentially spur the US on to more. The goal, bundled in from a looping deflection, was originally flagged offside but overturned on review. (See footer for my thoughts on the offside rule, which I have many thoughts about.)

The second half felt like a different game entirely. The US lost some of their earlier confidence while Australia saw more of the ball and more of the chances. Defensive mistakes and sustained pressure threatened to give Australia a route back into the match, but the home nation held firm and took their second win of the tournament.
footer:
A note on the offside rule

Now, this isn't really the place to air my views on the offside rule. Then again, it's my World Cup blog, so why not. Apologies in advance. I'll try to keep it light.
I have real issues with how "interfering with play" is interpreted. Firstly, if an attacking player can be deemed not to be interfering with play, why can't the same logic apply to defenders?

Secondly, and more pressingly if you happen to be the offside player: if you're running towards the keeper shoulder to shoulder with the player who will actually score the goal (and who was onside), you are absolutely interfering with play. The goalkeeper is having to work out which of you to prioritise. The defender is being blocked. How, precisely, is the second attacker not interfering with play?

Sorry. My editor has asked me to move on. If you'd like to continue this conversation, feel free to find me during a hydration break in the kitchen.

title: Last Gasp Germans
body:
When Ivory Coast went one up midway through the first half it was against expectations, if not against the run of play. They looked the sharper side, while Germany looked like the team that has fizzled out at a few tournaments too many in recent years.

But what makes champions isn't how they play when things are going well. It's how they cope when they're not.

Germany were second best for most of this game. Even after pulling level with half an hour remaining, Ivory Coast still looked like the team more likely to score next.

Chances came at both ends, but it was right at the death when Germany found the net and ended Ivory Coast's dream of a famous result.

Ivory Coast will spend the next few days rueing their squandered chances and replaying the closing minutes on a loop. Germany will be asking themselves some hard questions about what went wrong, and how you bounce back from winning so unconvincingly.

title: A Draw With a Winner
body:
Curaçao's first ever World Cup match last week was a baptism of fire. As predictable as that may have been, it will not have been enjoyable, and they will have spent the week trying to work out how to get something from their tournament.

Ecuador will have been equally disappointed not to take anything from their game against Ivory Coast, and came into this one looking to restore both pride and points. With Germany as their final opponents, they will have known that three points here were more or less essential if they harboured any hopes of making the last 32.

I've mentioned before that I've struggled to explain the beauty of a nil-nil draw to my wife, but I'm not sure it's something the American mind is built to comprehend. We've run into the same issue playing cooperative board games with her family, who struggle with the idea of games where nobody wins or loses. We tend to stick to Monopoly now, where  even if you make it to the point were someone wins, it still feels like everyone loses.

For a goalless draw, this one had a very clear winner and a very clear loser.

Curaçao will see a point in their first ever World Cup as a massive achievement. Ecuador will feel it more keenly than most defeats.

The Curaçao keeper will, if he's as into stats as I am, have complicated feelings about his new record for the most saves in a 90 minute World Cup match. He was magnificent, but will quietly wonder whether his defence could offer him a little more protection next time.

The game went largely as the scoreline suggests. Ecuador were the better side, had most of the play and most of the chances. Curaçao had a few opportunities of their own but it was largely a heroic rear guard action. With the qualification process being what it is, both teams still have a mathematical chance of making the next round, but even if they do somehow squeeze through I can't see either going much further.
footer:
A footnote on the Yeboah question

While watching Ecuador's first round match I was impressed with how well John Yeboah played, and found myself wondering whether he was related to, or possibly even the son of, Tony Yeboah, who played for Leeds in the 90s. Yeboah might be a common name in other parts of the world, but there aren't many in Guernsey, so it stood out.

It turns out that the Yeboah family are among the most secretive people on this planet. Even with my chief research assistant Ric on the case, our efforts came back inconclusive. 

Tony may or may not have children. If he does, he might have two or four, which might include some daughters. John might be the child of one of Tony's siblings, who may or may not exist. 

There is a very real chance the Yeboah enforcement team could be knocking on my door any moment to ask me to take this down.

title: Dutch Courage, Swedish Despair
body:
Fresh off their drubbing of Tunisia, Sweden would have gone into this match flying high. It didn't take long for the Dutch to clip their wings.

Looking like a completely different team to the one that drew with Japan, the Netherlands attackers had clearly worked out where each other were. Their darting runs and clinical passing were enough to slice the Swedish defence wide open.

When Cody Gakpo played the perfect cross for the opening goal, Aleksander Isak must have been wondering where that kind of service had been all season.

Sweden had a couple of half chances but the Netherlands were clearly the better side, and more goals felt like a matter of when. They added a second shortly after, but that was the extent of the scoring in the first half. It was end to end and great to watch but neither side was able to play the final ball.

The Sweden that returned after the hydration break had a new shape and a new lease of life. They looked sharper at the back and more dangerous going forward. Unfortunately they just couldn't convert their chances. The Dutch, on the other hand, seemed to be able to score at will.

When Sweden pulled one back to make it 4-1, I still felt like there was a chance they could make a game of it. It was such a free-flowing match, and scoring had become almost routine despite good defending on both sides. I realise that's a massive contradiction, but that's football.

A late Dutch goal felt superfluous, although the record books will no doubt enjoy the symmetry of a team winning and losing consecutive World Cup games by the same 5-1 scoreline.

Sweden go into their final group game knowing they need something from a capable but inconsistent Japanese side. 

Tunisia, meanwhile, will be contemplating what awaits them against the team who just thrashed the team that battered them in the first place. The maths is not encouraging.

title: The Big One-Triple-Zero
body:
This was the 1,000th World Cup game ever played. I love stats as much as the next person, probably considerably more, but I never quite know how to feel about this type of milestone. Was I supposed to send a card? Is this a cake situation? It feels like the answer to a really bad pub quiz question that only the one insufferable team in the corner knows, and they're already insufferably pleased about it.
The more important question is whether this was a game worthy of being the 1,000th World Cup match.
It was not.
For a 4-0 scoreline it was all a bit underwhelming. Japan were clearly the better side and could easily have scored another four. Tunisia offered little resistance and fewer chances. The Japanese goals, while generally well worked, had a tendency to go in despite the players' actions rather than because of them.
If in years to come some really boring person asks where you were for the 1,000th World Cup match, I hope you can truthfully answer "happily asleep in bed."

title: Nobody Told Cape Verde
body:
On paper, before the tournament started, this looked like a nothing fixture. Uruguay would have looked at their group and pencilled in an easy run to second place, with Cape Verde as a guaranteed three points. Watching Cape Verde against Spain might have given them pause, but small teams often produce one extraordinary performance against the big boys before quietly returning to their usual level.

From the first whistle it was clear this wasn't going to be a walkover.

When Cape Verde won a free kick from nothing a long way out, nobody expected anything from it. Instead, they scored their first ever World Cup goal. The Estadio, and presumably most people watching at home, did a double take.

Going behind shocked Uruguay into action, but it wasn't until the 44th minute that they made a dent. Remarkably, they scored again just minutes later and went into the dressing room 2-1 up and presumably feeling a good deal better about themselves.
Cape Verde came out after the break with fire in their bellies, but it took a catastrophic error from the Uruguayan keeper to make the difference. When he came sprinting past his own defenders to clear a loose ball 25 yards out, Cape Verde were quick to seize on the moment and were suddenly level again.

On the verge of something historic, Cape Verde retreated into desperate defending. Uruguay had chances, but even the introduction of the entirely legendary Darwin Nunez was unable to break the deadlock.

With only the hapless Saudi Arabia left to play, could Cape Verde somehow sneak out of the group stage and into the knockouts? And if so, is that an indictment of the 48 team World Cup, or the joy of it? Genuinely not sure.

title: Saudi's Long Afternoon
body:
After a very poor first outing, Spain needed to prove that their position as one of the tournament favourites wasn't entirely unjustified.

From the first whistle it was clear this was a different Spain side entirely. The question was no longer whether they would win, but by how many.

They were 3-0 up before the hydration break, and the Saudis left the pitch wishing it had been the final whistle instead.

The break had the effect of putting the brakes on the scoring. Spain continued to apply pressure but were unable to find the target again before the end of the half.

They made up for it quickly in the second, adding a fourth early on. Spain continued to create chances throughout but an acrobatic keeper proved equal to the task of keeping them out, and the game ended without them adding further to their tally.

Spain delivered a performance that showed exactly what they are capable of. Saudi Arabia showed that they are not quite ready for the biggest stage. Although, given eight years and home field advantage, they probably still won't be.

title: Belgian Waffle, Part Two
body:
We already established last week that I don't rate Belgium in their current form. I thought Iran would put up a solid fight but ultimately lose based purely on fitness levels.

For those who don't watch a lot of football, it's a common phenomenon that lower ranked teams playing top sides will often produce an impressive display for the first 70 minutes, at which point the difference in fitness and squad depth tends to make itself very obvious.

That's roughly what I was expecting. It's roughly what didn't happen.

For much of the game Belgium looked the better side, but Iran had the better chances. Belgium peppered the Iranian goal with shots, but it was the Iranian breaks on the counter that came closest to breaking the deadlock.

When Belgium went down to ten men midway through the second half they had been looking like the team most likely to score. The change in dynamic was immediate. Belgium dropped deep, Iran started to control the midfield, and Belgium's attacks looked even less threatening than they had before the red card. For most of the remaining minutes it felt like they had quietly decided to play for the draw and get out with a point.

There were chances at both ends, but when the whistle blew it ended in stalemate. Both teams will be relieved to have kept the ball out of their net, and disappointed they didn't take the 3 points.

title: A Finger in the Air
body:
A lot of pickers chose New Zealand. My belief is that this had less to do with footballing reasons and more to do with the soft spot many of us have for the island nation on the other side of the world, combined with the fact that they were very cheap.

Those crazy enough to be watching this live would have been delighted when New Zealand found the net fifteen minutes in, not entirely against the run of play.

My pre-tournament expectation for Egypt was simple: Salah's name was on the teamsheet, and that was all I needed to know. Like most of you, my research doesn't involve anything scientific. Confused memories, name recognition of anyone who's played in the Premier League, and a finger in the air. It's a system.

So I wasn't surprised when Egypt equalised.
Salah had a very different role here to the one he played for Liverpool this season, which mainly involved sitting on the bench looking annoyed about it. Given more freedom to find space, he looked involved even when he wasn't being directly influential.

When Egypt levelled, the result never really felt in doubt again. Salah started playing his greatest hits, picking up the ball on the right and cutting into the box, and unlike most Premier League teams this season, New Zealand had no answer for it. Salah duly got his name on the scoresheet.

It was his corner that set up Egypt's third, but it was a lost-looking New Zealand defence that really made the goal.

New Zealand's pickers will be disappointed, but perhaps not entirely surprised. Egypt, on the other hand, look exactly like the free-scoring, occasionally leaky side I was hoping for. Salah included.

title: Norse Code
body:
Some great players destroy opponents with size and strength. Some leave them standing with pace and vision. Others pick them apart with wizardly ball control.

Erling Haaland gets to pick and choose which option he fancies, and that's why he's possibly the most exciting player at this tournament.

Norway aren't a one man band. They have plenty of others creating chances and taking shots, and their first goal barely featured a cameo from the giant Viking. But everything feels more electric when Haaland is central to a move.

Despite Senegal pulling it back to 2-1, and again to 3-2 deep into injury time, this never really felt like ending any other way. Even when Senegal won a corner in the hundredth minute, it always felt like Norway's game to lose. They didn't lose it.

Senegal have shown some genuinely exciting attacking play, which makes their defensive frailties all the more frustrating. Dropped into almost any other group they would have been nailed on for second place and automatic qualification. Instead they find themselves needing a win and a generous pinch of luck to stay in the tournament.

title: A half of two games
body:
Not content with splitting matches into four quarters, last night the Americans tried splitting the game into two entirely separate ones.
Luckily for France, both episodes starred Mbappé.
Around fifteen minutes in, the ball found him on the edge of the box and he curled a stunning left-footed shot into the top corner. I don't know whether it's the ball, the quality of the players shooting, or the questionable reflexes of the keepers, but there have been a remarkable number of great long-range goals so far. I have absolutely no complaints about this.
With half time approaching, the heavens opened and both teams played out the remainder of the half without really troubling either keeper.
America's lightning laws kept the players in the dressing room considerably longer than the usual fifteen minute break, and it must have felt like an entirely new match when they reappeared over an hour later.
If you were feeling generous you could blame what followed on the extended break. But whatever the cause, when your goalkeeper fumbles a sloppy back pass directly into the path of an attacking player, there is very little chance you're not going to be punished for it. France duly obliged.
The second half felt one-sided and lacked the energy that comes from a genuinely competitive game. Iraq fashioned a couple of chances but were never going to do anything with them. France added a third, secured their place in the knockouts, and moved on to what will hopefully be a sterner test in their final group game.

title: Messi: Human After All
body:
A lot of the teams at this World Cup are like movie franchises. Yes, there's a whole team there, but it's built around a star. That star is the one people come to see, the driving force behind the whole production, the person who carries the weight of everyone's expectations.

Argentina are Mission Impossible, and Messi is Tom Cruise. You may not like the guy, but you cannot deny he's doing extraordinary things for someone his age.

Messi started the match on the verge of claiming the record for the most goals scored in World Cup history. When VAR spotted a mistimed challenge in the Austria box, he got his chance from the spot.
What followed was proof that even the great Messi is not immune to trying a stupid penalty routine and missing it.

For most of the game Argentina were the better side, but it was also a bit dull. No matter how enthusiastically the commentators talked up Austria, they were always the second best team on the pitch, with few chances and even less conviction in front of goal.
Argentina continued to dominate but struggled to find a way through. Eventually, after several attempts to set up teammates, Messi decided to take matters into his own hands. He waltzed across the six yard box looking for a shooting position, couldn't find one, and shot anyway. It went in. Of course it did.

A laboured performance from an Argentina side that dazzled, at least for one half, last week. Austria are still well placed to qualify but will need something from their final game to avoid the complicated third place algorithm that nobody fully understands, including, probably, FIFA.
footer:
A footnote on movie franchises
Obviously in this analogy, Portugal are the Indiana Jones films. Harrison Ford used to be a great action star, and although he was a bit grumpy and reluctant to put himself out for the team, he had the talent to carry a movie. He's still grumpy. But putting him in charge of an action franchise at this point feels cruel to him, the rest of the cast, and the paying public.

title: Long shorts, short memories
body:
Late in the second half, the commentator started discussing a qualification controversy from a World Cup 44 years ago, when two teams played out a result they both knew would see them through to the next round.

My mind drifted to the black and white footage and the long shorts of the 1960s. When he clarified it was actually 1982, I had to count on my fingers to confirm I was really that old.

It was guaranteed that one of these teams would claim their first ever World Cup points today, and against the run of play it was Jordan who staked the first claim.

Unfortunately for them, Algeria scored twice from corners in the final twenty minutes, securing the points and at least third place in the group. 

Algeria and Austria now go into their final group game against each other knowing that neither can top the group, but that a draw would all but guarantee them both a place in the knockouts.
Luckily, there is absolutely no history of corruption in FIFA.

title: Nil Nil, Deservedly
body:
A wise person probably once said there are no easy games at the World Cup. They were lying, but it's one of those things that makes you sound like you know what you're talking about.

Ghana set up in a low block, which is a term that seems to have been invented in the last couple of years but I believe means sitting back and defending like your lives depend on it.

For the entirety of the first half, this is exactly what Ghana did. They sat back, absorbed pressure, and England seemed completely unable to figure out what to do about it.

We all hoped England would return after half time with the same attitude shift they showed against Croatia. If anything, it was Ghana who came out with more ambition.

As the rain continued to pour, both sides found more opportunity to attack. England pushed forward in numbers, leaving space at the back for Ghana to fashion chances on the counter.

The commentators kept promising England would improve when they made substitutions, then maybe after the hydration break. England kept doing the same thing with the same result.

Ghana created two solid chances in behind the England defence, and in my opinion deserved a free kick from the first and possibly a penalty from the second. England got away with both and kept searching for an opening that never came.

It wasn't until the 85th minute that England had their first real chance. They pushed hard until the final whistle, but never looked like a team that deserved anything from this game.

They didn't get anything from this game.

title: Panama's Last Stand
body:
After their defeat to England, Croatia knew they needed three points here, both to settle the nerves and to ease their path to the knockouts. Panama were never going to make it straightforward.

The first half was goalless but not without chances, and more than half of those fell to Panama, who came very close to breaking the deadlock on more than one occasion.

In the second half Croatia were quickest out of the blocks, and their superior quality told with a brilliantly worked goal. That, however, was the extent of their ambition. For the remainder of the half they suffered under a torrent of attacks as Panama pushed desperately for an equaliser, going close several times.

A little sloppiness here and some last-ditch stops there were enough to see Croatia over the line. Panama finished disappointed, and have joined the growing queue of teams booking their flights home.

title: Five Goals and a Grudging Admission
body:
When I turned on the TV just before half time and saw Portugal were 3-0 up, I was disappointed. When I found out He Who Should Not Be Playing had scored two of them, I was distraught.

Watching the goals back at half time, it was clear he had actually worked for both, making intelligent and well-timed runs into space for teammates to find him, then placing the ball into the net with considerable precision.

I can only assume, and will continue to believe, that he spent the other 43 minutes of the half sitting in a deck chair complaining about something.

Over the following 45 minutes Portugal added two more to their tally, which means He Who Should Not Be Playing scored less than half of his team's goals. Loser.

Uzbekistan had a few chances but there was only ever going to be one result here.

Portugal looked like a completely different team tonight. And this time, Ronaldo was playing on the same side as his teammates.
footer:
A footnote, with reluctance

As much as it pains me to say it, Ronaldo is able to do extraordinary things with a football, and it's genuinely great to see him join the list of big names who are performing when it matters. I reserve the right to take this back at any moment.

title: Chances Galore, Goals: One
body:
Another game of plenty of chances but only one goal to show for it.

Both keepers pulled off heroics to keep their sides in it, but in the end it was a well-placed shot from Muñoz that wrong-footed the DR Congo keeper and snuck in at the near post.

Colombia go into their top of the table clash with Portugal knowing they have already guaranteed progression, and that even a point will see them top the group.

DR Congo will need a win and for other results to go their way, but will still believe they have a good chance of sneaking through.

title: Don't Do Anything Stupid (They Did)
body:
Scotland went into this match needing something. A win felt unlikely but a draw would do. If they had to lose, at least keep it close. The game plan was simple: stay calm, keep the ball, don't do anything stupid.

That lasted approximately six minutes, until they handed Brazil the ball in front of goal and Vinícius Jr. scored probably the easiest goal he'll get all tournament.

The calamity was nearly doubled twenty minutes later when they gifted him a second opening and he calmly slid it past the keeper. Luckily for the boys in blue it was chalked off for offside, and Scottish hopes survived a little longer.
Unfortunately they didn't even make it to half time before Brazil doubled their lead.

A quarter of an hour into the second half Brazil made it three, and the result which had felt inevitable from the sixth minute was confirmed. Scotland kept fighting for something, and at times it looked like they might squeeze a goal out of the stone. But Alisson in the Brazilian goal was reliably up to the job of keeping them out.

Brazil continue to grow into the tournament, though we'll have to see how they fare against sterner opposition in the knockouts.
Scotland will now need to sit back, watch the other results come in, and hope the mathematics works out in their favour.

title: Haiti to See Them Go
body:
Of all the teams heading home after the group stage, Haiti are probably one of those who deserve the most sympathy. They came, they competed, and on more than one occasion they made you forget they were the underdogs.

An early goal felt like a just reward for a side that had refused to simply turn up and make up the numbers throughout the tournament. For a brief, glorious moment, anything felt possible.
The Moroccan equaliser felt like a resumption of normal service. Then, four minutes later, Haiti blasted in a wonder goal and normal service was suspended again.

Haiti were still celebrating when Morocco equalised for the second time, and from that point the result felt inevitable. The gulf between the two sides showed itself clearly in the second half, with Morocco controlling the game and Haiti running out of both legs and luck. The goals took a while to come, but eventually they did, and the final scoreline felt like a fair reflection of how the game had played out.

It's a shame this game was played at crazy o'clock, as it looked like one of the best matches of the tournament so far.

Haiti will go home with their heads held high, having given this tournament considerably more than anyone expected of them.
Morocco, meanwhile, will hope they can keep pushing deep into the competition. Whether they can repeat the extraordinary run to the semi-finals they produced last time around feels unlikely, but on this evidence they are not a side anyone will be looking forward to facing.

title: Top 35 Goals of the Tournament
body:
Looking at the two 8pm games, this felt like the obvious choice. I was wrong, at least for the first 75 minutes.

The first half in particular was dull. Neither team looked like troubling the keeper, and as goals went in on the other channels I seriously considered switching over but hoped the second half delivered something worth staying for.

Switzerland came out after half time with a bit more of a game plan and were two up before Canada got their lumberjack shirts on. The Swiss continued to look the better side until the hydration break, at which point the Canadian substitutes changed the tune entirely.

Canada scored one of my top 35 goals of the tournament so far, and from that point didn't let Switzerland get a look in for the remainder of the match. They peppered the goal with crosses, but the Swiss keeper was equal to everything thrown at him. Another five minutes of injury time and they would probably have nicked an equaliser.

Both teams progress, and I find myself wondering whether Canada will become the first host nation to play a World Cup game in another country?

title: Everything to Play For, BiH Play Best
body:
A win for either of these teams would likely be enough to secure a place in the knockouts. A draw would send them both home. There was everything to play for.
Bosnia and Herzegovina were fastest out of the blocks and were almost one up before they'd finished clearing the giant flags off the pitch.
Chances came at both ends, but it was BiH who looked the more likely to convert. When the first goal arrived, it was an absolute stunner.

The second followed shortly after, a squared ball deflected into the net off a Qatari defender. Not pretty, but they all count.

BiH continued to create chances, but it was Qatar who next got their name on the scoresheet late in the half, and suddenly a comeback felt like a real possibility.

Both sides had their moments in the second half, but it was BiH who found the net again and sealed their place in the next round.

title: Why I'm in 136th Place
body:
Czechia were one of the cheaper teams I thought might surprise people at this World Cup. This is why I am currently sitting in 136th place.

Mexico have now played three, won three, scored six, and conceded zero. You can only beat the teams you're drawn against, but Mexico have made very neat work of the group stage and have secured a home tie in the next round.

Despite what the scoreline suggests, this felt like their toughest test so far. Czechia knew they needed a win and went all out in the first half, but never looked like finding a way through. Mexico were content to sit back, absorb the pressure, and break in numbers. Czechia's defence were unable to cope with those counter-attacks and gifted Mexico the first two of their three goals.

The game was over well before the third went in. Mexico will be satisfied with another composed performance.

Czechia go home disappointed, having never quite found the gear that would have made them the surprise package I, and presumably a handful of other optimists, were hoping for.

title: South Korea: Still Dawdling
body:
At the start of the tournament South Korea felt nailed on to cruise through this group, but they have struggled in every match. They would have gone into this one relatively confident of securing at least the point they needed against a side who have looked equally unconvincing in an already average group.

It was South Africa who clearly wanted it more.

South Korea barely got a look in and were under siege for much of the game. The goal, when it came, was deserved and not remotely surprising.

Going a goal down you might have expected South Korea to find some urgency. Instead they continued to dawdle politely towards defeat.

South Africa progress to the knockout stages for the first time in their history, which is a genuinely wonderful story, even if few would fancy their chances of going much further. South Korea will need other results to go their way, and even if they do make it through, they will be unlikely to make it past the last 32.

title: The Dream Dies, With Dignity
body:
I spent most of the first half of this game wishing I was watching the other one.

Taking a step back: when I booted up iPlayer I was given a choice of two games. The supporter of underdogs in me wanted to see if Curaçao could pick up the points they needed to make it through. Yes, they've been solid at the back, but could they throw men forward and actually score goals? Could they become the lowest ranked team ever to make it through to the knockouts?

The answer, it turns out, was no.

Although Curaçao started brightly, it was Ivory Coast who got on the scoresheet first, when the Curaçao defence combined to gift them an easy tap-in. Like Scotland the day before, you cannot start a game with a defensive error when you desperately need a massive result.

Both sides continued to push but rarely looked like adding to their tally. Ivory Coast's second goal didn't so much come against the run of play as against the general indifference of the match.

Ivory Coast go through in second place. Curaçao go home. They will be disappointed, but can be genuinely proud of the performances they put in throughout this tournament. As debuts go, there have been worse.

title: Momentum: Interrupted
body:
Germany went into this game guaranteed to top the group but will have wanted a solid performance to keep their momentum going into the knockouts.
Ecuador needed a win to stay in the tournament.
Scotland, meanwhile, were sitting in their hotel supporting a German side in a way they haven't done since the last time they played England.
It took less than two minutes for Germany to get on the scoresheet, and I'm sure they were expecting a comfortable evening right up until the moment Ecuador equalised two minutes later.

Germany spent the rest of the half bullying their way forward, trying to force another goal, but no matter how much Ecuador wobbled they held firm.

Germany thought they had the upper hand in the second half when they were awarded a penalty for a clear foul. When it was overturned and pulled back for an earlier foul the other way, Ecuador finally woke up and remembered they had the option to attack as well.
From that point Ecuador had most of the play, but neither side really looked like creating anything that would separate them, and a draw felt like where things were heading.

Germany appeared to have settled for a point when Ecuador snuck in a very late corner to take all three. It wasn't enough to earn them automatic qualification, but it should be enough to secure a third place spot and a place in the next round.
Scotland can now check the other scores.

title: Land of the Rising Yawn
body:
On paper, Group F looked like it would be one of the most evenly matched. With most third place teams getting through to the next round, the countries involved will have looked at their fixtures with a touch of nervousness, but with quiet confidence that they could do enough.

Sweden, after the rollercoaster of their first two matches, played the first half like a team that had collectively decided they deserved a rest. Japan seemed perfectly happy to join them.

There was a brief flurry of excitement in the second half when both teams fashioned themselves a genuinely good goal, before things settled back into their more sedate rhythm.

Sweden will feel they had the better chances and failed to take them. Third place will likely be enough to get them through, but both teams will hope to raise their game in the next round.

title: Brobbey, Oh Mr Brobbey
body:
Tunisia came into this match with nothing to play for, but that didn't stop them from fashioning the first chance.

Unfortunately, their buoyant mood lasted less than three minutes before a deflected own goal had them chasing the game. When Brian Brobbey stroked in a second four minutes later, the result felt all but sealed.

Both sides had chances, though the Dutch took the lion's share. When Tunisia pulled it back to 2-1 it felt more like a consolation than the start of a comeback. Another own goal restored the two-goal margin and settled matters.

This was always going to be a difficult group for Tunisia. Sloppy defending has cost them heavily throughout, and they simply haven't had the quality up front to compensate. They go home having contributed to some entertaining football, largely by leaving the back door open.
footer:
A footnote on Brian Brobbey
I may be alone in this, but every time our friend Brian pops up I can't help but hear Noel Edmonds saying "Brobbey brobbey brobbey," or find myself quietly humming "Brobbey, oh Mr Brobbey" to no one in particular. I do wonder if we could convince the Netherlands to try a pink shirt with yellow spots as an away strip. The campaign starts here.

title: Turkey, not just for Christmas
body:
The last of the three hosts went into their final group game with first place already secured. Turkey, on the other hand, knew they were going home regardless of the result. The stakes, for one side at least, couldn't have been lower.

When the US scored three minutes in it felt like they would continue their free-scoring ways and coast to a comfortable send-off.

The narrative around this tournament has been that the USA is falling in love with football again. I'm not really in a position to confirm or deny this, as I'm not entirely sure my in-laws even know the World Cup is on. But I can see why a high-scoring national team might help win over a country used to sports where the goals flow considerably more freely.

When Turkey equalised on ten minutes, US supporters would have seen it as a blip rather than a concern. Every great sports movie needs a moment where the hero stumbles before coming back to triumph in the final seconds. Spoiler alert: the last-minute heroics were coming. Just not from the side anyone expected.

When Turkey scored again midway through the second half, this still felt like part of the same script. Adversity is an important beat in any sports movie, and the US had the lion's share of the chances, so there wasn't really anything to worry about.
An equaliser in the 59th minute was perhaps a little early to build proper tension, but it felt deserved given the pressure the US had been applying.

They continued to create and squander chances. I'm not entirely sure how that fits into my movie metaphor, although all those misses might work well as a  montage.

As any good producer would have hoped, it came down to the last kick of the game. The fact that it was Turkey who scored it was a twist worthy of The Sixth Sense.

The result didn't change the outcome. Turkey go home having won the battle but lost the war, the US move on having lost the battle but won everything that mattered. Which, now I think about it, makes this feel less like a standalone movie and more like the middle part of a trilogy. The hero takes a beating, learns some lessons, and lives to fight another day. The real test is still to come. Roll on the knockouts.
footer:
A footnote on naming conventions
For some reason the US team is almost always referred to as the USMNT, or United States Men's National Team, which is a little wordy for me. Though I suppose it is a practical way to distinguish them from their infinitely more successful USWNT. Perhaps it explains why "The Lions" has crept into common usage for the England men's team in recent years, as a way of borrowing some reflected glory from the more recently successful Lionesses.

title: Functional
body:
Both sides went into this game knowing that a draw would be enough to get them through to the next round. Somehow, remarkably, we ended up with a nil-nil draw.

Paraguay had the marginally better chances, but neither side lit the match up, and if you were looking for evidence that footballers are capable of reading a room, here it was.

Both teams progress. Nobody will remember this game.

title: Norwegian Wood (This Game Was Good)
body:
This was one of those games that could have gone one of two ways. As a quarter final this could have been a classic, two of the tournament's top teams hunting for glory. As a final group game with almost nothing left to play for beyond pride, it could have been a turgid slog.

When Mbappé nearly cracked the bar with an explosive shot early on, it felt like we were getting the former.

The commentators had barely finished gushing when, five minutes later, France opened their account with another great strike. Norway looked briefly lost, the French pressure too intense, and it seemed like they might double their tally before the vikings picked up their oars and rowed their way back into the game.

The momentum swung back and forth, but we didn't have to wait long for France to extend their lead. For many teams that would have been the moment to drop their heads. Norway went the other way and scored before we'd even finished watching the replays.
They continued to push for an equaliser, but it was at the other end where the problems lay. Disorganised defending gifted France, and specifically Dembélé, their third.

Norway, who in their defence were resting several key players, came out after the break desperate to salvage something and were handed a chance from the penalty spot almost immediately.
It was dreadfully taken. The two-goal canyon remained.

The pace went out of the game after that. Norway had the majority of the ball and the chances, but this bird had flown. France reminded them of that fact with a fourth deep into injury time.

So we got a little of both. Would a knockout meeting between these two, both at full strength, end the same way? I think the outcome would be similar, but the score would be closer.
footer:
The bad joke graveyard
While watching this match I had a bad pun and an ageing pop culture reference circling my brain looking for somewhere to land. I couldn't find the right place for either of them, and rather than save them for a later round I thought I'd just bury them here, where they belong.
I don't think a French supporter would sit down in the middle of Times Square and pretend to row. But a Norwegian would.
Also, is Bob short for Jane? Woof.

title: Ten Men, No Chance
body:
Senegal came into this match needing points and hoping for goals.

Iraq just wanted to save their deposit.

It was one-sided from the first whistle, and Senegal were one up by the third minute. When Iraq went down to ten men shortly after, you could see in their faces that they knew it was going to be a very long night.

As if to lull Iraq into a false sense of security, Senegal let them dangle in the web for the rest of the first half, teasing with potential but never quite finding the right finish.

In the second half they closed the trap, and the goals started flowing.

Senegal have looked good going forward throughout the tournament, but it's been at the back where they've struggled. Iraq were never going to test them in that regard, and they did what they needed to do. Whether it will be enough to see them through, they'll have to wait and find out.

title: Tehran Briefly Celebrated
body:
With Mo Salah leading the line I always thought Egypt would score goals, and when they got on the scoresheet five minutes in I felt thoroughly vindicated.

Little did I know that was about as good as it was going to get for them.

When Egypt gave away a sloppy penalty a few minutes later, you could sense the mood in the stadium shift to something excited but anxious. This World Cup has seen several spot kicks go begging, with even the biggest names missing, and there is no one on the Iran team who could be considered one of the biggest names. A combination of a poor penalty and a good save meant Iran were still chasing.

And chase they did. It wasn't long before the unsuccessful penalty taker redeemed themselves, pouncing on a spilled shot and firing it into the top of the net from a tight angle to pull things level.
For the rest of the game Iran always looked the more likely to score, but a combination of finishing issues and an acrobatic Egyptian keeper kept things level.

When Iran scored in the dying minutes the whole team, nay the whole country, was celebrating. The VAR check took so long that they had already started the open top bus parade back in Tehran.
Unfortunately the scorer was deemed offside, and despite further chances the game ended in a draw.

Looking at the third place table it appears Iran should still squeeze through, and given the way they've played and the adversity they've faced throughout this tournament, few teams on that list deserve it more.

title: Nerves Settled, Doubts Remain
body:
Belgium have performed poorly so far in this World Cup and went into the final group game needing a win, not just to qualify, though a draw might have been enough, but to settle their nerves and prove they actually deserve to be here.

It took 28 minutes, an overturned penalty, and a shot cleared off the line before Belgium finally got the goal they needed. They continued to push but it wasn't until the second half that they scored only their third goal of the tournament and doubled their lead.

With the result seemingly settled and New Zealand legs looking increasingly weary, Belgium kept squandering chances in a manner that has become something of a habit.
