The match changed pace in the final third. De Bruyne added a third before New Zealand pulled one back, briefly threatening to make things interesting. As if to stamp out any such thoughts, Belgium added two more, completing the rout against a New Zealand side who looked increasingly out of their depth as the game wore on.

Even with a performance like this, I still have my doubts about Belgium's ability to go deep in the knockout stages. They've spent three games making hard work of a very manageable group. The real tests are still to come.

title: Cape Able
body:
Cape Verde went into this game as the tournament's most surprising story, on the verge of making the knockout rounds on their first ever attempt. The fairy tale, it seemed, was still very much being written.

Saudi Arabia, meanwhile, knew that only a win would do. A draw suited Cape Verde perfectly, which is precisely the kind of information that tends to produce a certain type of football match.

What followed was one of those great nil-nil draws that I love to tell my wife about and that I'm amazed FIFA haven't banned for the sake of American television. Two teams, one of whom needed a goal and one of whom very much didn't, producing ninety minutes of structured tension that will have had casual viewers reaching for the remote and football purists nodding along quietly.
There were chances at both ends, though Cape Verde had the lion's share and always looked the more likely to make something of them. Whether through genuine defending, wayward finishing, or sheer collective willpower, the net remained undisturbed throughout.

In the end it didn't matter. Cape Verde sailed through to the knockouts in second place, their fairy tale very much intact. Saudi Arabia go home having never quite found the goal they needed.
Somewhere, my wife is shaking her head.

title: The Lethargy Continues
body:
Apart from a strong win against the mighty Saudi Arabia, Spain have underperformed so far in this tournament.

Uruguay needed something from this game, and having watched Spain over the last couple of weeks they might have quietly fancied their chances.

Spain rarely looked like they were dominating, but a snatched goal while several players from both teams rolled around on the floor was all they could conjure. As it turned out, it was all they needed.

Uruguay's injury time red card was too late to spur any sort of improvement from Spain and it ended 1-0.

Uruguay will be disappointed not to get more from this tournament. They had the tools to cause problems and never quite found the moment to use them. A deserved red card deep into injury time summed up their evening, though by that point it was far too late for Spain to take advantage even if they'd had the energy to try.

Spain will hope they find their rhythm before they run into someone who will punish their lethargy. Because that someone is coming.

title: I stayed up for this?
body:
There is talent in the England squad. These are footballers who produce solid, even exciting performances week in week out for their clubs. But put on an England shirt and they somehow become worse than the sum of their parts.

Whether that is down to the combination on the pitch, the game plan the coach is dictating, or the way opposition defences set up against them, I couldn't tell you. What I can tell you is that it makes watching them equal parts frustrating and boring.

Of England's three group games this one falls somewhere in the middle of the tedium scale. Unlike the Ghana game, where they never got out of first gear, at least here they gave us two moments of genuine excitement.

This wasn't like the magic ten minutes in the Croatia game, where England looked like the greatest team in the tournament. This was two moments of individual skill and desire rising briefly above the blandness that filled the rest of the performance.

England went into this game knowing that although they were already through, only a win would guarantee them top spot.

Panama had a few chances, mainly clustered in the third quarter, but were never really going to trouble a side that, on their day, is considerably better than this.

title: A Game Was Played
body:
With three teams going through from most groups, there were always going to be several games where both sides had already qualified, with only position left to play for.

When that happens, teams face a choice. Chasing potential favourable matchups is a rabbit hole best avoided, so you're probably better off either giving it everything or resting your big players. In a tournament with this many matches, managing playing time matters, but a sluggish defeat can be disastrous for that most mystical of forces: momentum.

Croatia decided to go for it in the first half. They had most of the chances, and in fact I'm not sure the highlights showed a Ghanaian player touching the ball until the final couple of minutes. Their goal after 30 minutes was deserved, though they would have hoped for more. As it turned out, they were going to need it.

Ghana came out of the tunnel like a completely different team and thoroughly deserved their equaliser. Their hopes of a positive result faded minutes later, however, when Croatia made it 2-1.

Was it the game of the tournament? No. Was the result worth the effort for either side? Also no.

Both teams progress, and that will be all that matters to them come Monday morning.

title: Six Goals and a Gentlemen's Agreement
body:
In a game where a draw would send both teams through but a defeat would send you home, the expectation was a cagey, careful affair with neither side taking any unnecessary risks.

Instead, they chose chaos.

A cheeky dink around the keeper put Austria ahead before Algeria had quite realised the match had started.

Algeria quickly understood what this meant and piled on the pressure. It took a moment of individual brilliance to wiggle through the box and pull them level, and from that point any pretence of a carefully managed draw went out of the window.

The second half, which we all expected to drift into comfortable mediocrity, instead swung wildly from side to side, with both teams taking it in turns to celebrate and fear for their tournament lives.

Austria regained the lead. Algeria pulled level again. Both sets of supporters aged several years.

Three minutes into injury time Algeria seized the lead for the first time in the match, and suddenly the unspoken gentlemen's agreement that had never really existed lay in pieces on the pitch.

But the game wasn't done. In the 96th minute Austria somehow found the net, and the shared celebration of mutual survival could finally resume.

If this was planned in any way, congratulations to the scriptwriters. I didn't see that twist coming, and I would happily watch a sequel.

title: Argentina's Secret Weapon (Not Very Secret)
body:
There was only ever going to be one result here. Jordan would have known that too, and will have gone into this game simply wanting to leave with their heads held high.

For a spell, they were doing exactly that. They defended well, limited their opponents' chances, but when they gifted a free kick to Argentina on the edge of the area it was the start of their downfall. Messi not being on the pitch didn't mean Argentina couldn't score Messi-like free kicks, as it turned out.

Jordan continued to defend resolutely against growing Argentine pressure, with both the crossbar and the keeper producing moments of genuine heroism. It couldn't last. A clear penalty gave Argentina their second, and the result felt settled.

Things had drifted a little in the second half when Jordan produced a brilliant team goal to give themselves something to celebrate. Whether Argentina were feeling nerves or had simply decided to introduce their main character, Messi arrived from the bench shortly after and, inevitably, found the back of the net almost immediately.

title: In some ways, aren't we all Columbian?
body:
I don't think I need to restate my feelings about certain members of the Portuguese squad, but unsurprisingly I watched this match wearing a metaphorical yellow shirt.

My boys in yellow were on top of this match for long stretches, as Portugal showed us the rather more pedestrian level of quality we remember from their first game, rather than the unexpected brilliance of their second.

I hadn't even noticed that He Who Should Not Be Playing was on the pitch until late in the first half, and that was only because he got in the way of one of his teammates.

Portugal had a few more chances in the second half, but it always felt like Colombia's game to win. The fact that they were unable to convert was more down to a lack of composure in front of goal than any real defensive pressure from Portugal.

Colombia thought they had stolen it right at the death, but a rogue toe was all it took for it to be ruled offside.

Another exciting nil-nil draw. You're welcome, America. Neither team really deserved more, though Colombia will feel they came closest to deserving something.

title: DR Congo Prescribes Three Goals
body:
Uzbekistan flew out of the blocks and had the ball in the back of the net mere seconds after the whistle. It was ruled out, but it didn't take much longer before they scored again, and this time it stood.

DR Congo had their own goal disallowed shortly after, when a VAR review spotted a hand to the face in the build-up. The officials were clearly having a busy evening.

It took a penalty midway through the second half for DR Congo to draw level. It came after a fairly even period of play and set up an exciting final stretch, with both teams knowing exactly what was at stake.

DR Congo needed just one more goal to make the knockouts. They got two.

A deft dink around the keeper and a well-placed shot into the corner settled the result and sent DR Congo through. Uzbekistan, who had given considerably more than most expected of them, go home.

The scoreline doesn't entirely reflect how much DR Congo struggled at times, and they will need a more convincing performance at the back if they are going to trouble the bigger sides in the knockouts. But in they go, and that's all that matters for now.

title: Not Much to Report
body:
After the last match I asked whether Canada would be the first host nation to play a World Cup game in a different country, hoping to generate some friendly discussion.

Unfortunately some wag decided to ask an AI co-pilot, and it turns out the answer is no.

And that concludes my TED talk on why AI is destroying the world.
I tell this dull little anecdote partly to distract from the fact that I don't really have anything to say about this match. I missed most of the first half due to an incident involving a potentially possessed toy, the details of which are a long story and frankly still unresolved, and I don't think I missed much.

The ball travelled quickly from end to end, but at no point did either side look like they had a coherent plan for getting it into the net.
The most surprising thing about the result was that there was one.

To be honest, I'm just glad it didn't go to extra time. An early night was worth more than any amount of additional football this particular evening.
footer: Co-pilot's claim that South Korea and Japan once played matches in each other's host country doesn't appear to hold up under some good old-fashioned googling. So this may be a rare case of Ric 1 - 0 Co-Pilot.

title: When a Habit Becomes a Tradition
body:
Germany were one of my more expensive picks. It came down to them or England, and I went with Germany for one simple reason. Germany have played really badly at the last two World Cups, crashing out of both in the group stages. I felt certain they'd do better this time.
And I was correct. Just.

Their opening game was a goal fest, and many would have seen it as a statement of intent. That now looks like an anomaly, and ever since they have looked considerably out of their depth.

For most of the first half they had the majority of the play but never looked comfortable or in control. And I don't just mean of the game. They rarely seemed in control of the ball itself.

Paraguay sat back and let the waves crash on them, then when the moment came, tore apart a flimsy looking German defence and found themselves one up going into half time.

Although Paraguay went close at the start of the second half, it was Germany who applied most of the pressure as they chased an equaliser. They found the back of the net with about half an hour to go, and it took a goalkeeping masterclass to stop them stealing the win outright.

In extra time Germany thought they had it, but the goal was ruled out for a foul on the keeper, and in the end penalties were needed to separate the two sides.

Germany turned out to be just as bad at penalties as they had been at the rest of the game, missing their first and third spot kicks and handing Paraguay a clear route through.

But the tension wasn't quite over. Nerves crept into the Paraguayan ranks too, and their fourth and fifth penalties were poorly struck. Had they let their chance slip? Were Germany somehow going to win another shootout against the odds?

Germany missed a third penalty, and the final Paraguayan taker slotted his away with a calmness I'm fairly sure he wasn't feeling on the inside.

Paraguay move on. Germany take an early shower yet again. Some habits, it seems, are very hard to break.

title: Worth Staying Up For (I Wouldn't Know)
body:
This was my pick for match of the round, and I woke up wondering whether the insomniacs had witnessed a classic, or whether my choice to sleep would be the one that turned out justified.

To be completely open with you, there was no way I could have stayed awake even if I'd wanted to, and I'm already thinking about what to do if England make it through to the last 16 and a 1am kick-off next Monday.

Based on the highlights, it looks like it was one of the good ones.

Both sides looked completely up for it, and while it took a long time for either to get on the scoresheet, it certainly wasn't down to a lack of trying.

It was 70 minutes in when the Netherlands rode their luck and a poor challenge to poke it past the keeper. As the minutes ticked down it felt like they would simply hold on, but Morocco's pressure finally found a way through, levelling it as the clock ticked past 90.

Morocco went close in extra time, but for the second time today penalties were needed to separate the two sides.

For both teams, the back of the net proved just as hard to find from the spot as it had been for the previous 120 minutes.

Following a masterclass in how not to take penalties, Morocco eventually squeezed out the result and moved on to the next round.

This was an unfortunate matchup for the Netherlands. There are plenty of teams still in the draw they would have comfortably beaten, though I suppose that would only have been delaying the inevitable.

title: One More, As Promised
body:
Brazil always play with the attitude that you can score as many goals as you want, because they'll just score one more.
Today that worked out. But only just.

Brazil had the majority of the play and the chances, but poor decision making and brilliant keeping meant they were unable to get on the scoresheet.

Thirty minutes in it was Japan who broke the deadlock. It came from some sloppy Brazilian midfield play that Japan pounced on, the midfielder dashing forward before placing it past Alisson and into the corner of the net.

Japan have a history of throwing away leads in World Cup knockout games and going out on penalties. When Brazil found the equaliser midway through the second half, the nightmare of spot kicks must have loomed large in the collective Japanese psyche.

It was the 96th minute when Brazil finally fulfilled their promise and scored their one more.

Japan pushed to recover but it wasn't to be, and they go home disappointed once again. One day the knockout curse will be broken. Today was not that day.

Brazil have had their moments but have not looked anywhere near their best. As they say though, winning is all that matters. At some point, someone better will call their bluff.

title: Vive la Difference (In Quality)
body:
If the full game was as one-sided as the highlights, the biggest surprise about this match is that the gap between the scores wasn't considerably higher. In the ten minutes they showed, Sweden had two attacks, one at the end of each half, and between them I don't think they amounted to half a chance.

At the other end, the Swedish defence seemed to offer no resistance whatsoever to the free-flowing French forwards. The fact that it took 43 minutes for Mbappé to add to his Golden Boot résumé was down to a lack of focus on the final ball rather than anything in a yellow shirt getting in his way.

In the second half France continued the assault with more precision. Two more goals, one again for Mbappé, meant the scoreline gave a fair impression of just how lopsided this match was.

Sweden go home having started the tournament promisingly before fading rather badly. Their opening result against Tunisia now looks considerably more flattering than it did at the time.

France continue to be the favourites to lift the trophy later this month, and on this evidence the billing feels justified. The one note of caution is that Sweden gave them far too much space and time, and against better opponents that luxury will disappear. France will need to be considerably more clinical when the chances come less frequently, because at some point in this tournament they will face a team that makes them work for every one.

title: Haal Mary
body:
Resting large numbers of players before big games always makes me nervous. Yes, it's probably the right call in a long tournament, but sometimes players lose momentum and struggle to rediscover their rhythm. Does it matter if your players are tired if they’re slumped in first class on an early flight home? 

That appeared to be the case for Norway here. After making ten changes for each of their last two matches, they looked slow out of the blocks, and for the first fifteen minutes struggled to contain a lively Ivory Coast side. Slowly, though, the Viking legs defrosted, and they began to go toe to toe with the Elephants.

When Norway took the lead close to the end of the first half it wasn't completely against the run of play, but it will have been a disappointment to Ivory Coast that they hadn't converted any of their earlier chances.

Norway continued their momentum through the third quarter, and it took a rousing hydration break speech and an inspired substitution to get Ivory Coast back into it.

Within minutes of Diallo entering the field, he had cleared a Norway chance off the line before making a brilliant run from the wing and drilling the ball into the net. From nothing, Ivory Coast were level.

Both teams continued to have chances, and extra time felt increasingly likely, when Haaland did what Haaland does and materialised in the box to bundle home a brilliant cross and snatch the win.

Norway row on to the next round. Ivory Coast head home after a positive tournament showing, and will be left wondering what might have happened had Diallo started the game.

title: Home Advantage, Well Used
body:
This was an important match for England fans, as the winner would be our next opponent should we beat DR Congo tonight. What we saw won’t have increased our confidence in the Lions chances of bringing it home.

Mexico have played like hosts throughout this tournament, consistently exceeding my expectations, and from the first whistle they looked by far the better side. When Ecuador had their first chance of the match twenty minutes in, Mexico, who could easily have been two or three up by that point, must have feared that their sloppiness in front of goal might come back to haunt them.

As if in response, they scored minutes later and never looked back. In the second half they added another, and although Ecuador fashioned half a chance late on, the result never looked like being anything other than a home victory.

The one bright spot in the Ecuador team was John Yeboah, who we all know is the son of a man whose family details remain classified. He was the only one who looked like getting anything out of this game for his side, but for most of the match the players in yellow were little more than training dummies for the Mexicans to run around.

A late red card for an Ecuadorian who covered his mouth while speaking was an unpleasant end to a match that had long since been decided. We'll never know what he said, but from the slow motion replay he didn't look like he was wishing the Mexican team good fortune in the coming rounds, or even sharing a recipe for the best gooey brownies. The players knew the rule going into the tournament, so he has no real argument against his dismissal.

Mexico march on and will be a formidable opponent for whoever has to travel to the heat, altitude, and noise of Mexico City early next Monday morning. Good luck to them. They'll need it.

title: Expected the Worst, Got Slightly Less Bad
body:
As a pessimistic England supporter I go into every England match expecting the worst, and still they find ways to disappoint me.

So far this tournament England have looked below average. The defence has looked weak, and there hasn't been anyone in the team who looks capable of creating a goal from nothing.

The England plan for this game was probably to avoid conceding, then sneak a goal or two early in the second half. Simple enough.

The script was rewritten within minutes when DR Congo found the gaping hole in England's non-existent defence and put themselves one up. It wasn't against the run of play. It wasn't out of nowhere. They were the better team and they punished an uncoordinated back four in exactly the way you'd expect.

Two key moments arrived late in the first half. A DR Congo player drove an open chance onto the England post, and then moments later Harry Kane went down in the box in what was either a blatant penalty or a dive worthy of a booking. I think in the Premier League it would have been given, and I'm surprised it wasn't even reviewed by VAR.

England created a few more chances, but the DR Congo keeper was having the game of his life and kept them from drawing level. The introduction of Saka, Gordon, and Eze changed things, opening up new routes past a tiring DR Congo defence.

There were fifteen minutes left when one of my fellow supporters stood up and announced: "If I go and get another glass of wine, the match will definitely go to extra time, so I have time to drink it." She had barely made it to the bar when Gordon found Kane, and amazingly England were level.

For the last fifteen minutes only one team looked like scoring, but none of us were confident it would actually happen. So when Gordon and Kane combined for a second time the room erupted with relief as much as excitement. A nail-biting ten minutes were all that stood between England and their place in the next round.

DR Congo deserved more from this game. England probably deserved less. But onwards we march, towards what I pessimistically believe will be a heavy defeat early on Monday morning.
footer:
A footnote on bank holidays
When Scotland had an early Sunday morning game a few weeks ago, King Charlie gave them all a bank holiday. So what I'm asking is: where's ours?

title: One Round Closer to Reality
body:
The group stage of this World Cup went really well for the majority shareholder of the hosts.

They scored plenty of goals and barely looked troubled. The relative lack of quality in their opponents was quietly brushed over, and US fans went into the knockouts believing they were now genuinely one of the better teams in the world.

There will have been some surprise in American households when the match kicked off and tiny BiH made it clear they weren't just there to play evil henchman number four in the USA's triumphant campaign.

BiH had the early chances before the US started to find their feet, and if they didn't quite dominate the rest of the half, they definitely looked the better side by the end of it.

Balogun, the USA's top scorer and talisman, put the ball in the net for the second time in the tournament, the first was clearly offside, just before the hydration break, and the crowd seemed to believe that was the win sealed.

Balogun was also involved in the biggest incident of the second half. A slightly wild attempt to win the ball in midfield, more clumsy than malicious, was deemed on review to be dangerous play. The straight red felt like a genuine shock to the casual US fans, who had already been given two lessons in the offside rule this game alone.

To be fair to the US, they put on a great display of how to play with ten men, and even managed to sneak a second. BiH came close in the dying minutes, but the US fans get to celebrate for at least one more round.

BiH go home feeling they could, and probably should, have got more from this match.

The US will likely go forward continuing in their deluded belief that they're going to win the whole thing. Whether Belgium provide a tougher test remains to be seen.

title: Golden Goal
body:
Belgium haven’t impressed so far in this tournament. It feels like one tournament too far for their golden generation.

Senegal have been genuinely exciting to watch, but while I thought they might get a couple of rounds into the knockouts, I didn’t believe they’d make it as far as the semi-finals.

For 85 minutes this game went exactly as I expected. Senegal were the better team. They had more of the play and created more chances. A goal at the start of either half, the second one of the finest of the tournament so far, meant they looked set to stroll through to the next round.

Perhaps that comfort bred a little overconfidence, and some sloppiness crept in that stopped them from properly capitalising on their dominance.

Belgium, meanwhile, looked genuinely lost. They were constantly a step behind the game and struggled to create anything of note. There didn’t seem to be a threat anywhere in the team. In a Premier League game, fans would have been heading for the exit trying to catch an earlier train.

Then, in the 85th minute, Belgium surprisingly fashioned one of their first real chances of the match and found the back of the net. Even more of a shock, two minutes later they repeated the feat.

Senegal were shell-shocked. It took most of extra time for them to properly come back to life and fashion a real response. They looked the more likely to find something, but as the seconds ticked down, penalties felt increasingly inevitable.

Belgium had the final chance of the game. As the ball skipped across the six yard box there was a clash of legs. Shouts went up from the Belgian players, and the referee was called to the screen.

the game was destined to end with a penalty but only Belgium were going to get the chance to take it.

Belgium snuck through by the finest of margins. Sometimes a team manages to stumble onto a slightly undeserved cup run, but stiffer opposition may well put an end to this one sooner rather than later.

Senegal, who were, then weren’t, but might still technically be African champions depending on who you ask, go home devastated. They will know they deserved considerably more from this game, but a spell of indecisiveness and a couple of careless minutes let it slip through their fingers.

title: Portugal Steal It, Again
body:
We're approaching the end of a golden generation, and I don't just mean the one currently mugging Belgium in real time.

There are a handful of names in this tournament who are almost certainly playing their last World Cup, the kind of players whose fame has long since escaped football and become part of the wider cultural furniture. The Beyoncé tier. One name, global recognition, no context required.

Luka Modrić has never quite reached that level. Ronaldo unquestionably has. Tonight was always going to be the final World Cup curtain for one of them, and football has a habit of making sure these moments actually feel like something.

Portugal dominated the first half and somehow found a way to waste most of it, missing chance after chance with the kind of profligacy that makes you wonder whether they'd actually read the script. Croatia, patient as ever, waited for the door to be left open and deservedly walked through it early in the second half.

From there the game stopped being a story about legacy and started being genuinely chaotic. Both sides traded chances, both found the net at various points only to have it chalked off, and the whole thing had the frantic, lawless energy of a match that had forgotten what it was supposed to be about.

Portugal levelled from the spot. Croatia continued to dominate but kept squandering the openings that mattered. As the clock ran down it felt less like a football match and more like a countdown to extra time, both sides seemingly resigned to another thirty minutes of this.
Then, in the 94th minute, entirely out of nowhere, Portugal scored. It felt less like a goal Portugal had earned and more like one they'd simply taken, the way a magician takes your watch while you're still applauding the last trick.

Croatia weren't done. In the 106th minute they thought they'd equalised, and for a few seconds the whole tie tipped back the other way, before the flag went up and the goal was ruled out. Devastating doesn't quite cover it.

Portugal continue to stumble through this tournament in exactly the way great champions sometimes do, never entirely convincing, always somehow still standing. Croatia go home rueing the chances they didn't take, aware that on another night, with a little more composure in front of goal, this would have been their story instead.

For Ronaldo, the curtain stays up a little longer. For Modrić, it falls here. Football rarely bothers to be sentimental about who gets which ending.
footer:
A footnote on Ronaldo, and the other Ronaldo

I'm still amazed at how quickly Ronaldo replaced Ronaldo as football's default answer to "the Ronaldo." For a while it felt like the original was cementing his place among the greats, not quite Pelé tier, but comfortably in football's second tier of gods. Then he more or less vanished, as suddenly as he'd arrived, and an entire generation grew up with no idea there was ever a different one.

title: No Golden Era, Just a Conveyor Belt
body:
Spain went into this having never looked troubled at this World Cup, though "impressive" would be pushing it. Solid, reliable, occasionally a bit dull — like a pension plan.

The bigger story is the squad itself. Over the past few years Spain have quietly kept restocking with young players, reframing the team on the fly, and somehow avoiding the golden-era-hits-a-cliff-edge moment that's currently mugging Belgium in real time.

Austria, meanwhile, muddled through the groups looking like they'd wandered into the newly expanded last 32 by accident and were hoping nobody would check their invite.

This one always had the feel of a foregone conclusion. Austria found their way into Spanish territory a few times, but at no point did it look like they were actually going to score — more sightseeing than threat. Spain, by contrast, couldn't stop creating chances; their only real concern was whether they'd bother finishing enough of them.

When the goals did come, they were thoroughly deserved. As is Spain's place in the next round.
footer:
A footnote on titles

I had 'Shouting Schlager, Schlager, Schlager' locked in as the title if Austria won. They didn't. So instead you get something much weaker, and this footnote as a small act of grief.

title: Structured. Disciplined. Forgettable.
body:
Nobody looked at the match schedule and thought "I must stay up for this one." Switzerland versus Algeria was never going to be the tournament's marquee fixture, and it never pretended otherwise.

When commentators start reaching for words like "structured" and "disciplined" to describe a performance, you already know what kind of night you're in for. Those are the words you use when nobody did anything wrong and nobody did anything memorable either, football's equivalent of a restaurant review that praises the parking.

Switzerland always looked the better side, in the quiet, methodical way that Switzerland tend to look better than most sides without anyone quite noticing how or when it happened. 

Algeria had their moments, threatened occasionally, and never once looked like they had the final ball to actually trouble the scoreline. A goal near the start of each half was enough to see the Swiss home, and at no point did it feel like anything other than business being taken care of.

This was the football equivalent of watching someone competently assemble flat-pack furniture. Efficient, unremarkable, and over before you really had a chance to get invested.

Switzerland move on, and will likely find life considerably harder against opposition that doesn't hand them quite so much time and space. Algeria go home having given a reasonable account of themselves without ever really threatening to change the outcome.
